content.assessment: Procedural admissions reform avoids directly abolishing the track structure, which may reduce the intensity of the coalition-founding constituencies' opposition relative to structural removal, but the divided union positions on the underlying track question suggest even a narrower admissions-only reform may not secure a unified labor-side coalition, and church-maintained schools sitting outside central administrative authority represent a coverage-limiting veto capacity rather than full opposition, weakening durable national implementation.
opportunities: Could be sequenced as a lower-salience first step that builds administrative capacity and trust before considering age-related reform
strengths: Leaves the symbolically charged track-age structure untouched, potentially avoiding the sharpest edge of opposition mobilization seen against structural change
Prior administrative desegregation precedent shows admissions-level intervention can shift outcomes even without abolishing tracks, offering some political proof-of-concept
threats: Precedent of prior desegregation measures 'stagnating mid-decade' suggests this reform type carries a documented reversal/fatigue risk even when initially adopted
weaknesses: Church-maintained and private schools' institutional independence from central authority creates a structural loophole limiting the reform's national coverage and effectiveness
Internally divided teacher unions (phase-out-supportive PSZ vs. broader-focus PDSZ) prevent a unified labor coalition from anchoring implementation support
